required.title: [For Student] A typing program (TypingClub, Typing.com, or equivalent)
[For Mentor] Hello Ruby: Adventures in Coding
required.author: Linda Liukas

suggested.title: [For Student] A simple word processor or text editor
[For Student] A drawing or art program

narration_prompts.detail: What is your current speed? How has it improved? What do you still need to work on?

narration_prompts.prompt: Show me how you organize your files.
narration_prompts.detail: Where do things go? How do you name them? Can you find a file from last week quickly?

narration_prompts.prompt: Show me a creative project you completed.
narration_prompts.detail: What did you make? What was the hardest part? How did you solve the problems you encountered? What would you do differently next time?

narration_prompts.prompt: Show me a Scratch program you built.
narration_prompts.detail: What does it do? Can you explain how it works — walk me through the code blocks? Did you encounter any bugs? How did you fix them?

narration_prompts.prompt: What is the difference between using a computer to watch things and using a computer to make things?
narration_prompts.detail: Which have you been doing more of?

cross_connections.description: The understanding that computers follow instructions leads directly to writing instructions for them in Scratch.
Digital writing projects reinforce the writing skills developed in 3.2. The student who writes stories on a computer is practicing both digital creation and composition.
4.2.4-A is the digital equivalent of 2.2's physical making skills. One builds with wood and metal; the other builds with code and digital tools. The builder mindset is the same.
Finishing projects requires discipline. Debugging requires patience. Touch typing requires consistent practice. These are IC2's habits applied to digital creation.

content.why_this_matters:
Most people use computers to watch things, read things, and play games that other people made. There is nothing wrong with that. But a computer can do much more — it can be a tool for making things. You can use a computer to write stories, draw pictures, make animations, build simple games, create presentations, and record videos. Once you start making things with computers instead of only watching things on them, everything changes. You become a creator, not just a consumer.

This packet is about building the habit of using computers to create. You will learn to type properly (a skill that saves you thousands of hours over your lifetime), organize your digital files, make creative projects, and write your first computer programs using blocks you can drag and snap together. The emphasis is on finishing things — completing a project from start to end, even if it is simple. A finished simple project teaches more than an unfinished complicated one.
content.what_this_develops:
1. **Touch typing** — proper technique that builds speed and accuracy as a lifelong physical skill
2. **File organization** — creating, naming, saving, and organizing files into a logical structure
3. **Creative project completion** — using a computer to make something and finish it
4. **Introduction to programming logic** — sequences, loops, and conditionals through block-based coding
5. **The builder mindset** — approaching computers as creation tools, not consumption devices
content.core_content:
### Part 1: Learning to Type — A Skill That Lasts a Lifetime

Typing is a physical skill — like playing piano or writing by hand. The earlier you learn proper technique, the more it benefits you. A person who types 60 words per minute spends less than half the time on written work compared to someone who types 30 words per minute. Over a lifetime, that difference adds up to thousands of hours.

**Proper technique matters:**
- Place your fingers on the home row (ASDF for the left hand, JKL; for the right)
- Each finger is responsible for specific keys
- Look at the screen, not the keyboard — this is called "touch typing"
- Speed comes with practice, not with rushing

**The practice schedule:** Typing practice should happen regularly — 10-15 minutes per day is more effective than an hour once a week. The typing program tracks your speed and accuracy. Set goals: if you currently type 15 words per minute, aim for 20 next month. Small, consistent improvement adds up.

Do not rush accuracy for speed. A fast typist who makes constant errors is not actually fast — they spend time fixing mistakes. Accuracy first, then speed.

### Part 2: Organizing Your Digital World

Everything on a computer is a file — documents, pictures, programs, videos. If you do not organize your files, you will not be able to find them later. Digital organization is like organizing a desk — if everything is in a pile, finding what you need takes forever.

**Folders are like drawers.** You create folders to group related files together. A good folder structure might look like:

- My Work
  - Writing
  - Art
  - School Projects
  - Scratch Programs
- Photos
- Downloads (to sort later)

**Naming matters.** "Document1" tells you nothing. "My Story About Dragons — March 2026" tells you exactly what the file is and when you made it. Good file names save future-you hours of searching.

**The habit:** Every time you create or save a file, put it in the right folder and give it a clear name. This takes ten seconds and saves ten minutes later.

### Part 3: Making Things — Creative Projects

The most important thing about a creative project is finishing it. A half-finished masterpiece teaches less than a completed simple project. Here is why: finishing requires you to solve every problem the project presents. When you quit halfway, you skip the hardest problems — which are usually the ones that teach you the most.

**Projects to try:**

- **Write a short story.** Use a word processor. Write a story with a beginning, middle, and end. Save it with a good name in your Writing folder. Print it out. You made something.
- **Create a digital drawing.** Use a drawing program to make a picture. It does not have to be perfect — it has to be finished.
- **Make a presentation.** Pick a topic you care about. Create a short presentation — 5-8 slides — with text and images. Present it to your family.
- **Record a short video.** Plan a 1-2 minute video. Record it. Watch it. You just produced content instead of consuming it.
- **Make a photo collage.** Arrange and edit photos into a collage using any simple editing tool.

**The builder mindset:** Every time you sit down at a computer, ask yourself: "Am I going to make something, or am I going to watch something?" Both are fine — but the habit of making is more valuable than the habit of watching. A computer is a creation tool first.

### Part 4: Your First Programs — Block-Based Coding with Scratch

In 4.2.1-A, you learned that computers follow instructions. Now you are going to write instructions for a computer — and see it do what you tell it to.

**Scratch** is a programming language designed for beginners. Instead of typing code, you snap together colorful blocks that represent instructions. Each block does something: move a character, play a sound, change the background, ask a question, repeat an action.

**Key concepts you will encounter:**

**Sequences:** Instructions happen in order. "Move 10 steps, then turn 90 degrees, then move 10 more steps" is a sequence. The order matters — changing the order changes what happens.

**Loops:** Sometimes you want to do something over and over. Instead of writing "move 10 steps" a hundred times, you use a loop: "Repeat 100 times: move 10 steps." Loops let you do repetitive things efficiently.

**Conditionals:** Sometimes you want the computer to make a choice. "If the character touches the edge, bounce back." "If the player presses the space bar, jump." Conditionals tell the computer: if this condition is true, do this thing.

**Variables:** A variable is a box that holds a value. You might create a variable called "score" that starts at zero and goes up by one every time the player catches something. Variables let your program keep track of things.

**Projects to try in Scratch:**
- An animation — characters moving and talking
- A simple game — catch falling objects, avoid obstacles, or solve a maze
- An interactive story — the reader makes choices that change what happens

**The key learning:** When your program does not work (and it will not, at first), that is not failure. That is debugging — finding and fixing the problem. Debugging teaches patience, logical thinking, and the habit of reading error messages carefully. Every programmer debugs constantly. It is part of the process, not a sign of doing it wrong.

### Part 5: The Builder Mindset

Here is the most important idea in this packet: a computer is raw material. Just as wood can be carved into a bowl or a toy, a computer can be shaped into a story, a drawing, a program, a song, or a video. The person who only consumes digital content is like someone who lives in a workshop but never picks up a tool.

You are a builder. From this point forward, creation should be part of how you use computers — not all of it, but part of it. Watch a video, then make one. Play a game, then build one. Read a story, then write one.

The habit of creating with digital tools compounds over time. The child who starts making things at age 7 has thousands of hours of creative practice by age 17. That practice shows — in their skills, in their confidence, and in their ability to solve problems by building solutions.

mentor_guide.competence_evaluation:
### 1. Typing Progress
**Developing well:** The child practices regularly and shows measurable improvement over weeks and months. They are developing touch typing — looking at the screen rather than the keyboard. Speed is age-appropriate and increasing.

**Developing slowly:** The child avoids practice or practices sporadically. They hunt-and-peck rather than using proper technique.

**Diagnostic questions:** What is their current speed? What was it a month ago? Are they looking at the screen or the keyboard?

### 2. File Organization
**Developing well:** The child creates folders, uses clear file names, and can find their own files quickly.

**Developing slowly:** Files are scattered, unnamed, or dumped in one location. The child cannot find work from previous weeks.

**Diagnostic questions:** "Find the story you wrote last week." If they can do it in under a minute, the system works.

### 3. Project Completion
**Developing well:** The child has completed at least two creative projects — finished, saved, and shareable. They can describe what they made and what was hard.

**Developing slowly:** The child starts projects but does not finish them. There are no completed artifacts.

**Diagnostic questions:** "Show me something you made and finished." If nothing exists, the focus must shift to completion.

### 4. Scratch Programming
**Developing well:** The child has built at least one working Scratch program and can explain how it works — walking through the blocks and describing what each does. They have debugged at least one problem.

**Developing slowly:** The child has explored Scratch but has not completed a program. They cannot explain what the blocks do.

**Diagnostic questions:** "Walk me through your program. What does this block do? What happens if I remove this one?"
